reviews.review_title: No smooth turn option in 2025 is silly
reviews.review_description:
The game itself looks like it could be interesting but while i can sort of live with teleport movement even though I hate it when you add the complete lack of smooth turning  it becomes a total deal breaker.
I will be refunding this. If at some point a smooth turn option is added to the game I might return someday.

reviews.review_title: Two Idiots in a Van and a Facility
reviews.review_description:
EDIT TO ADD: There are two tutorial videos on my YT channel to help you get started (@misschiefvr). A Wolf in VR also has a couple videos of his attempt at playing. 

-PLATFORM: VR headset + smartphone (no, "not" your iPad, Karen).
-GENRE: Co-op Puzzle Mayhem.
-MY CURRENT STATUS: stuck in a van, shouting at my friend again.

Let me just say it: Elsewhere Electric is my favorite panic-inducing, brain-melting, friendship-testing VR puzzler right now. It's not just a game, it's a communication skills exam disguised as a sci-fi escape room. And I’m here for it.

SETUP:

This is a two-player co-op experience. One player (the Installer) throws on the VR headset and stumbles blindly through a mysterious (very dark) facility, while the other (the Operator) sits safely in a van, tapping furiously on a smartphone like they’re trying to order pizza during a blackout.

Note: "Smartphone." Not tablet. Not iPad. Not your cousin’s weird Android phablet. A smartphone. Ask me how I know? (Hint: 45 minutes of confusion and one deeply regretted FaceTime session.) iPhone 12 and newer is needed to use the mobile app. I can’t speak for Android because I enjoy things like software updates, functioning group texts, and not accidentally opening the calculator instead of the camera. 

GAMEPLAY:

There’s no intro cinematic. No long-winded mission briefing. No mysterious narrator with a gravelly voice telling you “everything changed when the lights went out.” You're just... working here now. Congrats! It’s your first day, and corporate has left zero instructions. Classic.

From the get-go, you and your partner are surrounded by cryptic symbols, weird machines, and increasingly passive-aggressive equipment that refuses to function without the correct arcane button sequence. There is no hand-holding, unless you count yelling “WHAT DO YOU SEE?” every five seconds.

The Operator gets a (ancient) sci-fi dashboard full of buttons, toggles, and obscure data panels that definitely look like they do something. Spoiler: they do. Just maybe not what you "want" them to do. Don’t button mash. Trust me. This isn’t Mario Party. This is a serious workplace. Kind of.

The Installer, meanwhile, gets to walk around a maze of rooms, decoding ancient pictographs, reconfiguring doors, and trying not to panic when the ceiling whispers something at them (jk). Oh yeah, there are invisible creatures. They're technically harmless, but emotionally? They’re a lot. They don’t attack “you,” they just loom. And drink. They’re basically terrifying little emotional support cryptids.

DIFFICULTY:

This game is "hard." Like, “take notes, draw diagrams, question your self-worth” hard. You will need a notebook. Or an actual whiteboard, if you're feeling spicy. Some puzzles rely on pure observation; others are about syncing actions across devices. All of them will make you wonder why you didn’t go into accounting instead.

The symbols aren’t fixed. They’re procedurally generated for each profile. So if you rage-delete your profile and start over (no judgment), don’t expect the same symbols. The code changes. The puzzle gods are watching. 

That said, the moment you and your partner finally figure out what a button does, or decode one of the game’s many weird symbols, you’ll feel like you just invented electricity. Together.

FINAL THOUGHTS:

Elsewhere Electric is brutal, confusing, and absolutely brilliant. It throws you in the deep end and says, “Figure it out, nerd.” It’s not for the faint of heart or the short of temper, but if you and a friend are up for one of the most clever and genuinely "cooperative" VR games out there, it’s worth every shout, scribble, and scrambled clue.

Bring your brain. Bring your buddy. And for the love of the invisible drink demons, "bring a phone, not a tablet."

RATING: 4.9/5 — Couldn’t solve the final door, but now my friend and I communicate exclusively in obscure symbols. Worth it.

developer_response.body: It's almost as if this review was in the design meetings! It's so exciting to know that this quirky and challenging game struck a chord with you. Thanks so much for playing.

reviews.review_description:
For a game that has just released, it feels like it was made 10 years ago.

Snap turn only, teleport only, the sliders in the minimal settings did absolutely nothing, sliding both either way and pressing a random button / square next to the slider made no difference whatsoever.

Oh I also loaded into the game standing around 8 ft tall and couldnt change the height without reloading the game twice before I was my actual height.

There is zero help / tutorial. The person on the phone had no reason to swipe through to see more symbols he just happened to accidently do it.

Before leaving the elevator we went back up and back down before I realised I has to teleport across the room, when I did go up and back down it then shifted my height a ft taller (excellent)

The fact that this was 19.99 is absolutely mind blowing.

I will be refunding it shortly.

developer_response.body:
Definitely there are players who prefer smooth locomotion - the game is real-time networking between two devices, and VR player position is an important data point. Teleportation allows us to maintain synchronous positioning, but we'll mark this as an important feature for many. Sad to hear the setup took a few tries to calibrate - we'd be happy to troubleshoot on our Discord.

Elsewhere Electric is 'zero help' by design. Experimentation is how you master the game from the start, and the key is to carefully observe what's happening in your partner's environment. Accidents are great opportunities to discover a new feature of the game! I totally agree that this isn't the gameplay style for everyone, and we're doing our best to explain that it's a challenging 'no tutorial' style of play.

reviews.review_title: Bad attitude devs forgot about app part
reviews.review_description:
Really dissapointed in this game. The trailer put this game as a collaborative experience but it's more you figure out your part, wait for your partner to figure out theirs. Rinse and repeat...

We didn't get very far as the app is incredibly non-intuitive. As the vr user, I just sat in the dark for 30minutes while my phone partner pressed random buttons hoping to get any progress. There was also huuugeee desync issues and wouldn't communicate with the vr game. we had to reload the game to get the game to accept the app inputs. 

Extremely frustrating boring puzzles and the devs rotten responses to valid critiscm puts a nasty taste in your mouth. Buyers beware, it's not worth it.

developer_response.body:
Thanks for this feedback. We've tried to stress that this game is 'challenging' and 'requires teamwork', but it's valuable for players to hear that it can be frustrating.  It's valid criticism that the interface is non-intuitive, but understanding the systems is part of the puzzle game right from the beginning.  Pressing random buttons is not recommended, but experimentation early on is key - each button has a purpose and may only present through the other player's POV.

As always, our Discord is open to log any issues you may be experiencing (technical or for gameplay tips): https://discord.gg/BXFaeZhkjr

reviews.review_title: Fun smart time with my family and friend
reviews.review_description: A bit of a challange getting into the game as it has no tutorial but once you figure it out it’s an excellent escape room! Me and my kid really enjoyed figuring it out by ourselves .

I'm giving this three stars because I think the game is a great game and we're probably just idiots but this game is insanely confusing and I get that that's supposed to be a part of the game and that is kind of fun. But I think they should have at least added a tutorial or something because randomly the lights went out and we didn't know how to fix that and its pretty hard to navigate and I haven't played the game that much yet, but it's just very confusing. So that's why I'm giving it three stars but I do think it has potential if we figure it out. EDIT: we figured out the basics and it's a really fun game. It's much more enjoyable now.

developer_response.body: So happy to hear you came back to try again! Everyone in Elsewhere Electric starts off confused because there's no explanations - it sounds like you've crossed that threshold now. I promise the game will keep introducing new concepts as you progress, and once you've mastered the past challenges new ones will be waiting. Thanks so much for the review.

reviews.review_title: The Dark Souls of Puzzle Games?
reviews.review_description:
Elsewhere Electric isn’t here to hold your hand – it’s here to test your wits, your patience, and your ability to communicate with someone else under pressure. It throws you into the deep end and expects you and your co-op partner to be able to swim, or at least learn to swim pretty quickly! 

That might not sound like a selling point, but I love challenging puzzle games like The Witness and Blue Prince, so this game was definitely  made for me. When you add in the asymmetric co-op aspect it takes on an entirely different dimension that's essentially like a multi-level escape room set in a liminal, retro-future corporate nightmare. It looks amazing in VR and features some really cool and unsettling environmental storytelling that helps to build out the world.

The only other thing I would add is that you really need to choose your co-op partner wisely. This is not a quick pick-up-and-play party game like Keep Talking and Nobody Explodes! The two games are mechanically similar, but that's where the comparison ends. This is a deep and deeply rewarding puzzle game that requires two people to be engaged and on board in the same way that you'd need to be for an advanced escape room.

Communication is the most important part of Elsewhere Electric – and if you don't communicate well with your partner you will not have a good time. If you do communicate well with your partner though, the game is a brilliant and beautiful experience akin to learning a new language together – and one that rewards you with increasingly difficult puzzles and mysteries that you now have the skills to can solve together. If that sounds as interesting to you as it was for me, then Elsewhere Electric is absolutely worth your time.

developer_response.body: Haha can we steal this tagline? Obviously you've come to the right place with those references - thanks so much for playing!

reviews.review_description: This game sounds good, two players work together to communicate information and explore a facility. However this is not the case, first of all the game is very buggy, me and my friend had problems with the levels not starting or the app suddenly crashing leading to frustration. Second of all the game explains little to nothing which can work in some scenarios but when you have a remote with a ton of different things you can do, a small tutorial covering at least the basics would be great. All these problems make the game more frustrating than fun to play. In summary this game is not worth the $24.99.

developer_response.body: We'd love to hear what issues you're experiencing on our Discord - you may have found new ways to play we didn't anticipate! If you're finding it a bit confusing, try acting systematically with your partner - warn them you're going to try something, and then observe what's changed. (You may even want to write it down as you learn!) Sorry to hear it's been a frustrating experience for you.

reviews.review_title: Fun idea, major bug preventing progress.
reviews.review_description: The initial  gameplay was fun and interesting, but I hit a few road blocks. I streamed this game with a friend because I thought it would be a fun shared experience. Many times things went without explanation causing a lot of time where nothing got done. On floor 5 we encountered a game breaking bug. To our knowledge we needed the camera in the room and power in the room to open the door. When we got to the second room we couldn't move the camera to the room. We extracted to restart, but when we moved the camera to the second room, we couldn't open the door too it, and we couldn't move the camera back. The only way to fix that would have been to restart the entire game. Although it said we were only 10% of the way in, it was still 2 and a half confusing hours that we did not want to go back and do again. I want to like this game, but this bug has prevented me from enjoying it and progressing.

developer_response.body: You're already elite reaching Level 5 in our books! Level 5 is the first time these 'no going back' puzzles present themselves, and we had to design a RESET switch into the elevator for this exact reason. Below the button you both simultaneously press to engage the elevator, there will be a switch in VR. Pull that switch and your partner will need to confirm the reset for Level 5 - you'll keep all your progress up to that point. Thanks for reaching out and we're always happy to troubleshoot on our Discord here: https://discord.gg/BXFaeZhkjr

reviews.review_description:
This game is so impossibly hard without a proper tutorial. I get trying to figure it out is supposed to be the fun part but i’ve spent an hour in Level 3 and have made like no progress. There is no instruction on how to kill those wretched bugs and i can’t tell if my friend or the phone is just dumb or if this game is just dumb😭😭😭
Just please add a tutorial and i’ll change to 5 stars. Half the reviews just feel like devs or something leaving it to boost their stars. Probably don’t get this game because i can almost guarantee that you will get stuck early on and will never make progress.

Quick edit: It’s also very buggy with bad movement bad turning and clipping out of the 
map every 10 minutes. Don’t waste 25 bucks on this.

reviews.review_title: Great Game For Two (no single player)
reviews.review_description: This is a great game for two challenging you to work as a team with another person.  It is fun engaging and sometimes frustrating experience but when you get through that next steps you will truly feel like you accomplished something.  Highly recommended !

Asymmetric gameplay! Retro vibes! Play with my non-VR friends!  Check, check, and check. Sign me up.  We need more games like this that bring the sophistication and quality, with thoughtful design and hours of gameplay.  Yeah, the puzzles are hard, but you got this!  That's part of the fun.

I'm about 3 hours in. I have experienced no glitches or crashes. It's a unique and original game that requires consistent and frequent communication between two people. There's definitely a learning curve and I appreciate that the game doesn't hold your hand and instead requires you to figure things out. I'm giving it 5 stars for originality, and challenge. I hope to see more games like this.

developer_response.body: Thanks so much for playing! So great to hear you enjoyed this quirky challenge - there's a lot more left to solve.

reviews.review_description:
A two player only, hard as nails, weird, relationship killer. 

Will cause more divorces than Mario Kart.

It's pretty good 😎

developer_response.body: HAHAHA! I couldn't have said it better myself. Many players think the mobile phone portion is a casual game, but if anything the 'Guy in the chair' role has many more options available. Thanks for a great description and I hope everyone stays together!
